content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 22, 2012

Kevin B. Briggs
County Counsel
2220 Tulare Street, Suite 500
Fresno, CA 93721-2128

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-12-032

Dear Mr. Briggs:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Fresno County Supervisor Judy Case regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

Because your question is general in nature and does not refer to any specific governmental decision before your agency, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

May Supervisor Case take part in discussions and decisions of the Board of Supervisors regarding approving or denial of the Cemex project and certifying its E.I.R. when the result of the decisions on the Cemex project could affect her economic interests?

_________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-12-032
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

Supervisor Case may not take part in decisions of the Board of Supervisors regarding
approval or denial of the Cemex project and certifying its E.I.R. unless the there will be no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon her economic interests.

FACTS

You are the County Counsel for Fresno County. Supervisor Judy Case has asked you to
seek advice on her behalf. There is an imminent appeal to the Fresno County Board of
Supervisors of a Planning Commission Decision to either grant or deny an application by
Cemex. Cemex is seeking approval to mine Jesse Morrow Mountain in Fresno County. The
mine will produce rock and gravel (aggregate) and Cemex will produce and sell the products to
private companies, individuals, cities, counties, and districts. According to the E.I.R. for the
Cemex project, which must also be certified by the Board of Supervisors, the Cemex project will
generate hundreds of truck trips per day and will produce air pollution as a result of those trips
and the mining operations.

Supervisor Case's husband, Mr. McNairy, is a Certified Public Accountant and owns his
own accounting business.

Supervisor Case has a community property interest in income from the firm. Supervisor
Case reported clients of Mr. McNairy's firm as sources of income to her on her last Statement of
Economic Interests (Form 700). Each client was a source of income of over $10,000 or more to
her in the previous year. Among the sources were Van Groningen, Van-G trucking, and Sanger
Cold Storage. Supervisor Case's community property share in her husband's income from his
firm was between $100,000 and $1 million.

Roger Van Groningen is the President and owner of Van-G Logistics and owns Van-G
Trucking, Sanger Cold Storage and another cold storage facility in the City of Selma. Van-G
Logistics is a statewide trucking operation with five locations across California, including two
cold storage sites in Fresno County.

Mr. Van Groningen intends to expand a rail distribution center near Selma on 154 acres
located at the corner of East Coneio Avenue and South Peach Avenue. The County of Fresno,
through its Board of Supervisors on August 9, 2011, approved a general plan amendment that
would allow Van Groningen to expand the rail distribution center. Supervisor Case recused
herself from that decision without seeking advice from the FPPC.

The Van Groningen General Plan Amendment allows, by-right, various uses including
concrete and cement products production as well as ready-mix concrete production. The Van
Groningen General Plan Amendment imposes various requirements on the inception of such
uses, including a Site Plan Review, landscaping plans and provisions subject to County approval,
the irrevocable dedication of right of way for a future street, the requirement to participate in the

File No. I-12-032
Page No. 3


funding of on and off-site traffic improvements. The cost of on site improvements are estimated
in excess of $448,000. The costs of future off-site improvements are estimated at $105,200.
Thus a lot of development costs must be incurred before Van Groningen can begin distributing
products from that location.

Mr. Van Groningen has been quoted in the local press as saying that if the rail
distribution center is expanded by adding one 9,000 foot track:

1. The rail distribution center will be able to load 60 cars of fresh fruit and
vegetables, and unload 100 cars of aggregate per day.

2. The expansion of the rail distribution center could begin bringing in aggregate by
rail in 90 days.

3. The rail distribution center has the potential to put off the mining of aggregate at
the Jesse Morrow Mountain.

4. Transporting aggregate by rail from several locations within a 400 mile radius  could
supply all of Fresno County with all the aggregate it will ever need, providing    no need to go
forward with Jesse Morrow Mountain because of this alternative.

5. He already has suppliers in place.

6. He does not have a stance for or against the Cemex mine.

7. Van-G Logistics could in 6-9 months schedule trains carrying 80-100 cars of
aggregates on a continual basis and use of rail would save money, reduce carbon  dioxide
and reduce wear and tear on roads.

You ask if Supervisor Case would have a conflict of interest in voting upon the appeal to
the Fresno County Board of Supervisors of a Planning Commission Decision to either grant or
deny an application by Cemex that seeks approval to mine Jesse Morrow Mountain in Fresno
County when Supervisor Case has an economic interest in Mr. Van Groningen and his
companies. The success of the Cemex application could affect Mr. Van Groningen's rail
distribution center project in that if the Cemex application is approved, the rail distribution center
project would become less useful.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;

File No. I-12-032
Page No. 4


Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision.

Step One: Is the individual a "public official?"

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(1).) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency...." (Section 82048.) As a member of the Fresno County Board of Supervisors, Supervisor Case is a public official within the meaning of the Act.

Step Two: Is the official making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decision maker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

Supervisor Case will be making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision when taking part in decisions regarding the appeal of the Cemex project decision.

Step Three: What are the official's economic interests?

Of the economic interests recognized under the Act,³ those interests implicated by your account of the facts are the following:

Business Interests – A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) You have stated that Supervisor Case has received $500 or more from her husband's business and the clients of her husband's business and listed them as sources of income on her Statement of Economic Interests. Among those clients is Roger Van Groningen and his respective businesses.

Personal Finances – A public official will always have an economic interest in his or her personal finances. A governmental decision will have an effect on an official's economic

___________________
³ Our analysis is limited to the economic interests you have identified.

File No. I-12-032
Page No. 5


interest in his or her personal finances if the decision will result in the personal expenses,
income, assets or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or
decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Step Four: Are the official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?

Business Interests – Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a person, including business
entities and sources of income, is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency
when that source of income, either directly or by an agent:

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."

Such interests that are not directly involved in governmental decisions under the rules
quoted above are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulations 18704(a) and 18704.1(b).)

The decision regarding the Cemex project was not initiated by Supervisor Case's sources
of income or by her husband's business, nor are they parties in, or subject of, the proceeding
within the meaning of Regulation 18704.1. Barring additional facts, it appears that Roger Van
Groningen and Mr. McNairy's business are indirectly involved in the decisions.

Personal Finances – An official's economic interest in his or her personal finances is
deemed to be directly involved in the governmental decision if facts suggest any financial effect
on the economic interest. (Regulation 18704.5.) It does appear that the success or failure of the
Cemex project would affect Roger Van Groningen and his business. The amount of business
that Van Groningen has may increase or decrease his need for accounting services. Therefore,
Supervisor Case's personal finances would be directly involved in the decision regarding Cemex
because the income her and her husband receive from her husband's accounting firm would
likely be affected.

File No. I-12-032
Page No. 6


Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on the official's economic interests?

Materiality

Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality
standard appropriate to each economic interest.

Business Entities- You have not provided the net income for Mr. McNairy's accounting firm in
which Supervisor Case has an economic interest. However, you have stated that Supervisor
Case's community property interest in that business' income could have been up to $1 million.
Therefore the materiality standards for a business that is indirectly involved in a governmental
decision can be found in Regulation 18705.1 and the applicable standard is determined by the net
income of the business from its most recent fiscal year.

If the accounting firm had net income of no less than $2.5 million, the financial effect of
a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease to the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $500,000 or
more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for
a fiscal year in the amount of $200,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of assets or liabilities of $500,000 or more."

If the accounting firm's most recent fiscal year had net income of no less than $750,000,
the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it is
reasonably foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease to the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $300,000 or
more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for
a fiscal year in the amount of $100,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
value of assets or liabilities of $300,000 or more."

File No. I-12-032
Page No. 7


If Mr. McNairy's accounting firm does not fall within the above listed criteria please refer to Regulation 18705.1 (copy enclosed) for the applicable materiality standard.

Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, is deemed material. (Regulation 18705.3).

You noted that the Cemex decision will affect Roger Van Groningen's plans since, while not the subject of the Cemex decision, it is an alternative to the Cemex project. If Mr. Thus, if the foreseeable effect on Mr. Van Groningen will reach the thresholds in Regulation 18705.1, which it appears it will, the supervisor will have a conflict of interest.

Personal Finances – An effect on an official's personal finances is material as stated in Regulation 18705.5(a), which provides:

"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period. When determining whether a governmental decision has a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a financial effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the official, nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment interest shall be considered."

Foreseeability

Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra, at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby, supra), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official.

If Roger Van Groningen or his business are indirectly involved Supervisor Case will need to determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that any of the above criteria listed in

File No. I-12-032
Page No. 8


Regulation 18705.1 will be met. You have not provided enough facts for us to make that
determination.

Any financial effect on a source of income directly involved in a governmental decision
is deemed material. You have provided facts indicating a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on Supervisor Case's economic interest in Mr. Van Groningen and his businesses
as a source of income because the Cemex decision may be an alternative to the Van Groningen
project. If that is the case, Supervisor Case may not make, participate in making, or influence a
decision regarding the Cemex appeal unless she can rebut the presumption of materiality by
showing that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decisions will have any financial effect, even
"one-penny," on Mr. Van Groningen or his businesses that are sources of income to Supervisor
Case.

Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?

Segmentation

The Commission has advised that "[u]nder certain circumstances, a public official
disqualified from one decision may participate in other related decisions provided that the
official's participation does not affect the decision in which he or she has a conflict of interest."
(In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) In these cases, related decisions may be segmented from
the decision in which the official has a conflict of interest so long as the action taken in the
related decision will not effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of the decision
in which the official has a conflict of interest.

Regulation 18709 provides the rules for "segmenting" a governmental decision:

"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial
interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following
conditions apply:

"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down
into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;

"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from
the other decisions;

"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's
participation in any way; and

File No. I-12-032
Page No. 9

"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made,
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of or
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified.

"(5) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably interrelated' when
the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the
result of another decision."

To the extent that decisions relating to Roger Van Groningen or his businesses are
considered first, without Supervisor Case's participation, and later decisions regarding Cemex
will not act to determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the previous decisions, it is possible to segment
decisions. If Supervisor Case needs additional assistance regarding the segmentation of a
specific governmental decision, it is advisable that she seek further advice.

Public Generally & Legally Required Participation

Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).)

Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to take
part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest
under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very
specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from acting. (Section
87101; Regulation 18708.)

However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to Supervisor Case's circumstances, so
we will not address them further.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

Enclosure

sections.analysis:
'-
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within 
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;  
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding 
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision. 
Step One: Is the individual a "public official?" 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." {Sections 
87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(b)(l).) A "public officiěl" is "every member, officer, employee 
or consultant of a state or local government agency .... " (Section 82048.) As a member of the 
Fresno County Board of Supervisors, Supervisor Case is a public official within the meaning of 
the Act. 
Step Two: Is the official making, participating in making, or influencing a 
governmental decision? 
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the 
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her 
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her 
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a governmental decision" when, 
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive 
review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decision maker 
regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to 
use his or her official position to influence a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the 
official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her 
agency. {Regulation 18702.3.) 
Supervisor Case will be making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental 
decision when taking part in decisions regarding the appeal of the Cemex project decision. 
Step Three: What are the official's economic interests? 
Of the economic interests recognized under the Act,3 those interests implicated by your 
account of the facts are the following: 
Business Interests - A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, 
including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the 
decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) You have stated that Supervisor Case has 
received $500 or more from her husband's business and the clients of her husband's business and 
listed them as sources of income on her Statement of Economic Interests. Among those clients is 
Roger 'van Groningen and his respective businesses. 
1• 
Personal Finances - A public official will always have an economic interest in his or her 
personal finances. A governmental decision will have an effect on an official's economic 
3 Our analysis is limited to the economic interests you have identified.  
interest in his or her personal finances if the decision will result in the personal expenses, 
income, assets or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or 
decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.) 
Step Four: Are the official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the 
decision? 
Business Interests - Regulation 18704.l(a) states that a person, including business 
entities and sources of income, is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency 
when that source of income, either directly or by an agent: 
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an 
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; 
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the 
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a 
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or 
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
subject person." 
Such interests that are not directly involved in governmental decisions under the rules 
quoted above are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulations 18704(a) and 18704.l(b).) 
The decision regarding the Cemex project was not initiated by Supervisor Case's sources 
of income or by her husband's business, nor are they parties in, or subject of, the proceeding 
within the meaning of Regulation 18704. l. Barring additional facts, it appears that Roger Van 
Groningen and Mr. McNairy's business are indirectly involved in the decisions. 
Personal Finances - An official's economic interest in his or her personal finances is 
deemed to be directly involved in the governmental decision if facts suggest any financial effect 
on the economic interest. (Regulation 18704.5.) It does appear that the success or failure of the 
Cemex project would affect Roger Van Groningen and his business. The amount of business 
that Van Groningen has may increase or decrease his need for accounting services. Therefore, 
Supervisor Case's personal finances would be directly involved in the decision regarding Cemex 
because the income her and her husband receive from her husband's accounting firm would 
likely be affected. 
'·  
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on the official's economic interests? 
l\!lateriality 
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each interest 
is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality 
standard appropriate to each economic interest. 
Business Entities- You have not provided the net income for Mr. McNairy' s accounting firm in 
which Supervisor Case has an economic interest. However, you have stated that Supervisor

Therefore the materiality standards for a business that is indirectly involved in a governmental 
decision can be found in Regulation 18705.1 and the applicable standard is determined by the net 
income of the business from its most recent fiscal year. 
If the accounting firm had net income of no less than $2.5 million, the financial effect of 
a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that: 
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease to the 
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $500,000 or 
more; or, 
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or 
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for 
a fiscal year in the amount of $200,000 or more; or, 
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the 
value of assets or liabilities of $500,000 or more." 
If the accounting firm's most recent fiscal year had net income of no less than $750,000, 
the financial effect of a governmental decision on the business Ĝntity is material if it is

more; or. 
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or 
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for 
a fiscal year in the amount of $100,000 or more; or. 
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the 
value of assets or liabilities of $300,000 or more."  
If Mr. McNairy's accounting firm does not fall within the above listed criteria please 
refer to Regulation 18705.1 (copy enclosed) for the applicable materiality standard. 
Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a 
public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, is deemed 
material. (Regulation 18705.3). 
You noted that the Cemex decision will affect Roger Van Groningen's plans since, while 
not the subject of the Cemex decision, it is an alternative to the Cemex project. If Mr. Thus, if 
the foreseeable effect on Mr. Van Groningen will reach the thresholds in Regulation 18705.1, 
which it appears it will, the supervisor will have a conflict of interest. 
Personal Finances-An effect on an official's personal finances is material as stated in 
Regulation 18705.5(a), which provides: 
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal finances 
is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period. When determining 
whether a governmental decision has a material financial effect on a public 
official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a financial 
effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the official, 
nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and 
liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect 
investment interest shall be considered." 
Foreseeability 
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each of his 
or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reas·onably foreseeable" 
that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably 
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met 
as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain 
to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re 
Tlwmer (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) 
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is 
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (bz re Tlwmer, supra, at 198.) 
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby, 
supra), the foreseeability 'or a particular financial effect is a determination that must be left, in 
most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official. 
If Roger Van Groningen or his businesses are indirectly involved Supervisor Case will 
need to determine if it is reasonably foreseeable that any of the above criteria listed in  
Regulation 18705.1 will be met. You have not provided enough facts for us to make that 
determination. 
Any financial effect on a source of income directly involved in a governmental decision 
is deemed material. You have provided facts indicating a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on Supervisor Case's economic interest in Mr. Van Groningen and his businesses 
as a source of income because the Cemex decision may be an alternative to the Van Groningen 
project. If that is the case, Supervisor Case may not make, participate in making, or influence a 
decision regarding the Cemex appeal unless she can rebut the presumption of materiality by 
showing that it is not reasonably foreseeable the decisions will have any financial effect, even 
"one-penny," on Mr. Van Groningen or his businesses that are sources of income to Supervisor 
Case. 
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception 
to the conflict-of-interest rules? 
Segmentation 
The Commission has advised that "[u]nder certain circumstances, a public official 
disqualified from one decision may participate in other related decisions provided that the 
official's participation does not affect the decision in which he or she has a conflict of interest." 
(In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) In these cases, related decisions may be segmented from 
the decision in which the official has a conflict of interest so long as the action taken in the 
related decision will not effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of the decision 
in which the official has a conflict of interest. 
Regulation 18709 provides the rules for "segmenting" a governmental decision: 
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a puQlic official has a financial 
interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following 
conditions apply: 
"(l) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down 
into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in 
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from 
the other decisions; 
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first 
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified o(ficial's 
participation in any way; and  
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, 
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of or 
otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified. 
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably interrelated' when 
the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the 
result of another decision." 
To the extent that decisions relating to Roger Van Groningen or his businesses are 
considered first, without Supervisor Case's participation, and later decisions regarding Cemex 
will not act to determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the previous decisions, it is possible to segment 
decisions. If Supervisor Case needs additional assistance regarding the segmentation of a 
specific governmental decision, it is advisable that she seek further advice. 
Public Generally & Legally Required Participation 
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the 
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is 
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally. 
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).) 
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to take 
part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest 
under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very 
specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from acting. (Section 
87101; Regulation 18708.) 
However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the 
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to Supervisor Case's circumstances, so 
we will not address them further.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Supervisor Case take part in discussions and decisions of the Board of Supervisors 
regarding approving or denial of the Cemex project and certifying its E.I.R. when the result of 
the decisions on the Cemex project could affect her economic interests? 
'·z Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation I 8329(c)(3).)

CONCLUSION: Supervisor Case may not talce part in decisions of the Board of Supervisors regarding 
approval or denial of the Cemex project and certifying its E.I.R. unless the there will be no 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon her economic interests.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 May 22, 2012 Kevin B. Briggs County Counsel 2220 Tulare Street, Suite 500 Fresno, CA 93721-2128 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-12-032 Dear Mr. Briggs: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Fresno County Supervisor Judy Case regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. Because your question is general in nature and does not refer to any specific governmental decision before your agency, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² QUESTION May Supervisor Case take part in discussions and decisions of the Board of Supervisors regarding approving or denial of the Cemex project and certifying its E.I.R. when the result of the decisions on the Cemex project could affect her economic interests? _________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) File No. I-12-032 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION Supervisor Case may not take part in decisions of the Board of Supervisors regarding approval or denial of the Cemex project and certifying its E.I.R. unless the there will be no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon her economic interests. FACTS You are the County Counsel for Fresno County. Supervisor Judy Case has asked you to seek advice on her behalf. There is an imminent appeal to the Fresno County Board of Supervisors of a Planning Commission Decision to either grant or deny an application by Cemex. Cemex is seeking approval to mine Jesse Morrow Mountain in Fresno County. The mine will produce rock and gravel (aggregate) and Cemex will produce and sell the products to private companies, individuals, cities, counties, and districts. According to the E.I.R. for the Cemex project, which must also be certified by the Board of Supervisors, the Cemex project will generate hundreds of truck trips per day and will produce air pollution as a result of those trips and the mining operations. Supervisor Case's husband, Mr. McNairy, is a Certified Public Accountant and owns his own accounting business. Supervisor Case
